title: Sworn Relation of Caillou

sections.body:
Inspector Lambert Caillou, of the very police force investigating the death, presented himself as a witness with some reluctance. He stated that he had visited Sauval at seven-oh-five for a "friendly call"—the two had known each other for years. He drank a cognac poured from the same decanter from which Sauval served himself. He left at seven-fifty-five, and Sauval was then alive and "in his usual sour humor." He denied any knowledge of a bribery ledger or a sealed filing cabinet.

The phrasing is too neat by half, which in murder-books is seldom a happy omen. The constables disputed the matter till dawn: whether the witness erred, dissembled, or sought by falsehood to shield a greater villainy. The desk-note beneath it read only: "keep apart from the louder papers."

A scrap of paper, found later in Caillou's office waste-basket, bore a pencilled note: sixth bell, seventh stair, fifth lantern, eighth berth.

subtitle: Entered as sober truth, though truth oft weareth a counterfeit coat.
